title: Stab-sewn album
subtitle: A corner-stab photo album sewn through the full stack with a running stitch
excerpt: A stab-sewn album holds a stack of single heavy sheets together with a running stitch through holes pierced near the left edge. No folded signatures are needed. The pages lie completely flat, making it ideal for mounting photographs, prints, or artwork.
sourceNotes: Stab sewing through a full stack (as opposed to the traditional Japanese stab-binding over folded signatures) is documented in hand-bookbinding manuals of the twentieth century as a simplified album structure. Technique synthesised from general hand-bookbinding practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A binding method in which a needle and thread pass through holes pierced from front to back through a full stack of pages rather than through the fold of individual signatures.

content.text: to bind a stack of heavy sheets by stitching through holes pierced from front to back near the left edge. The method requires no folding, no paste, and no more tools than a needle, thread, and awl. Because the pages are single sheets rather than folded signatures, they lie completely flat when the book is opened, which is why the format suits photographs, prints, and loose artwork.

items.name: Heavy paper or card for pages
items.description: 160-200 gsm cartridge or watercolour paper (slug: watercolour-paper-coldpress). Single sheets; not folded. Cut to identical size. Between 20 and 40 sheets makes a workable stack.

items.name: Cover card
items.description: Two pieces of 1.5 mm bookbinding board (slug: bookbinding-board-1-5mm) or heavyweight card, cut the same size as the pages. Can be left plain or covered in paper or cloth.

items.name: Bookbinding thread, waxed linen (slug: bookbinding-thread-linen)
items.description: 4-cord weight. Length: four times the book height plus 200 mm.

items.name: Bone folder (slug: bone-folder)
items.description: For squaring the stack before clamping.

items.name: Bulldog clips or book press (slug: book-press)
items.description: For holding the stack in register while piercing and sewing.

content.text: Stack the pages in order between the front and back cover boards. Tap the head edge on the table to level all sheets and square the stack using the bone folder. Clamp the stack firmly near the right edge with bulldog clips so the spine edge is accessible.

Mark the sewing stations on the spine edge with a pencil. Four stations work for most album heights. Place stations 15 mm from the head, 15 mm from the tail, and two equally spaced stations between. Pierce through the full stack with the awl at each station, working the awl straight through from front to back with a slow twisting motion.

Thread the needle and wax the thread. Enter the first station from the front, leaving a 100 mm tail. Pass to the second station and come back through to the front. Continue in this running stitch to the last station.

At the last station, turn and sew back in the opposite direction, filling in the gaps of the first pass. The result is a solid line of stitching on both front and back faces. Finish by tying off the working thread to the starting tail with a square knot at the first station, pulling snug before knotting.

Trim the thread tails to 5 mm. The album does not need pressing because no adhesive was used. The pages will not open fully flat; the spine edge has a natural resistance from the sewing. For photographs, mount using photo corners rather than adhesive so images can be repositioned.

The cover boards can be covered in hand-marbled or patterned paper before assembling the stack. A decorative running stitch using two threads of contrasting colour, sewn in opposite directions, produces a distinctive surface pattern on the spine. For very thick stacks, use a Japanese stab binding pattern rather than a plain running stitch.
